coredata.dc:title: 2D ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor for tracking of cells proliferation and metabolism
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Jun
Ding, Changqin
Zhou, Jie
Tian, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Extracellular pH plays a vital role no matter in physiological or pathological studies. In this work, a hydrogel, CD@Nile-FITC@Gel (Gel sensor), entrapping the ratiometric fluorescent probe CD@Nile-FITC was developed. The Gel sensor was successfully used for real-time extracellular pH monitoring. In the case of CD@Nile-FITC, pH-sensitive fluorescent dye fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) was chosen as the response signal for H+ and Nile blue chloride (Nile) as the reference signal. The developed fluorescent probe exhibited high selectivity for pH over other metal ions and amino acids. Meanwhile, the carbon-dots-based inorganic-organic probe demonstrated excellent photostability against long-term light illumination. In order to study the extracellular pH change in processes of cell proliferation and metabolism, CD@Nile-FITC probe was entrapped in sodium alginate gel and consequently formed CD@Nile-FITC@Gel. The MTT assay showed low cytotoxicity of the Gel and the pH titration indicated that it could monitor the pH fluctuations linearly and rapidly within the pH range of 6.0–9.0, which is valuable for physiological pH determination. As expected, the real-time bioimaging of the probe was successfully achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PH sensor
Hydrogel
Live cells
Ratiometric
Fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs, CD@ FITC-Nile, and CD@FITC-Nile@gel

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) TEM images of CDs. Inset shows lattice spacing of CDs. B) FT-IR spectra of (a) the as-prepared CDs, (b) Nile, (c) FITC, and (d) CD@FITC-Nile probe. C) Fluorescence emission spectra of (a) CD@FITC-Nile probe, (b) CDs, (c) FITC, and (d) Nile. D) Fluorescence spectra of CD@FITC-Nile probe (0.1mgmL−1) with the increasing of pH in the range of 5.00–9.90.
A and B) TEM images of CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor A) without probes and B) with probes. C and D) Confocal microscopic images of CD@ FITC-Nile@Gel sensor.

A) Plot of ratio (I green/I red) versus pH value for CD@FITC-Nile@Gel. Error bars denote standard error measurements (S.E.M) and obtained by three parallel experiments. B) and C) Selectivity experiments of CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor towards (B) metal ions (10−3M for Na+, K+, Ca2+, and 10−5 M for Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and Cu2+) and (C) various amino acids (Cys, Glu, Val, Iso, Ieu, Lys, Meth, Ser, Thr, His of 10−5 M). Black bars, grey bars and white bars represent measuring results in pH of 9.0, 7.4, and 5.0, respectively. D) pH reversibility study of CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor between pH 4.5, 7.4, and 9.0.

A) Photostability of the CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor measured by confocal microscope for 1h. B) The influence of oxygen toward the sensor. C) The influence of temperature toward the sensor. D) Cells viability values estimated by MTT proliferation tests. HeLa cells were in the presence of 100μL 3% CD@-FITC-Nile@Gel sensor for 24h (black) and 48h (white) at 37°C, respectively.

A) Confocal fluorescent images (pseudocolor) in the process of cells proliferation and metabolism. B) Relationship between ratio (I green/I red) and time of cells proliferation and metabolism. Error bars represent standard error measurements.
Working principle of the ratiometric fluorescent CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor for pH.

head.ce:title: 2D ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor for tracking of cells proliferation and metabolism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tongji University, Siping Road 1239, Shanghai 200092, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, East China Normal University, North Zhongshan Road 3663, Shanghai 200062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, East China Normal University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, East China Normal University, North Zhongshan Road 3663, Shanghai 200062, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Extracellular pH plays a vital role no matter in physiological or pathological studies. In this work, a hydrogel, CD@Nile-FITC@Gel (Gel sensor), entrapping the ratiometric fluorescent probe CD@Nile-FITC was developed. The Gel sensor was successfully used for real-time extracellular pH monitoring. In the case of CD@Nile-FITC, pH-sensitive fluorescent dye fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) was chosen as the response signal for H+ and Nile blue chloride (Nile) as the reference signal. The developed fluorescent probe exhibited high selectivity for pH over other metal ions and amino acids. Meanwhile, the carbon-dots-based inorganic-organic probe demonstrated excellent photostability against long-term light illumination. In order to study the extracellular pH change in processes of cell proliferation and metabolism, CD@Nile-FITC probe was entrapped in sodium alginate gel and consequently formed CD@Nile-FITC@Gel. The MTT assay showed low cytotoxicity of the Gel and the pH titration indicated that it could monitor the pH fluctuations linearly and rapidly within the pH range of 6.0–9.0, which is valuable for physiological pH determination. As expected, the real-time bioimaging of the probe was successfully achieved.

ce:section.ce:para:
Over the past 20 years, the role of intracellular pH played in the regulation of various cellular activities has been the focus of a considerable body of work. For example, intracellular pH plays a pivotal on receptor-mediated signal transduction, calcium regulation, ion transport, cell volume regulation, vesicle trafficking, cell growth and apoptosis, cellular metabolism, and cell adhesion (Gerson. et al., 1982; Golovina. and Blaustein. 1997; Loiselle. and Casey. 2003; Choi. et al., 2011; Gottlieb. et al., 1996; Kong. et al., 2012). An increase in cytosolic pH is responsible for the increase in DNA and protein synthesis, enhanced metabolic rate accompanying oocyte fertilization. In addition, the maintenance of a resting intracellular pH of 6.8–7.3 and alternations in intracellular pH accompanying the cellular events outlined are considered primarily to the result of an energy-requiring, active proton-extrusion system (Helmlinger. et al., 1997; Lardner. 2001). On the other hand, extracellular pH has actually found to be very important in biological system. Many cellular processes, for example, ion transmission, enzymatic activity, synthesis of DNA and protein, calcium ion levels, have been reported to be extracellular pH-sensitive. In addition, extracellular pH influences immune system by affecting leukocytes, neutrophils, macrophages and lymphocytes (Helmlinger. et al., 1997; Lardner, 2001; Mahnensmith. and Aronson. 1985; Kraus. and Wolf. 1996). More interestingly, scientists have recently found that dysregulated pH is emerging as a hallmark of cancer because cancers show a “reversed” pH gradient with a constitutively increased intracellular pH that is higher than the extracellular pH. A decreased extracellular pH limits dynamic HCO3
− -dependent buffering, promotes extracellular matrix remodeling and stimulates acid-activated proteases to facilitate tumor cell invasion and dissemination (Webb. et al., 2011; Hanahan and Weinbeig. 2011). So, tools to study 2D pH distribution are needed to gain new insights into complex biological processes. However, it is still a challenge for real-time tracking extracellular pH changes in the processes of live cells proliferation and metabolism, although a lot of methods have been reported for determination of intracellular pH over the past decades (Meier. et al., 2011; Medintz. et al., 2010; Albertazzi. et al., 2010; Schremla. et al., 2011; Srikun. et al., 2011; Chen. et al., 2013; Tantama. et al., 2011; Ding and Tian. 2015; Wan. et al., 2014).

So far, several analytical methods have been developed for determination of extracellular pH such as positron emission tomography (PET) radiotraces, magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS), (magnetic resonance imaging) MRI, and fluorescent imaging (Zhang. et al., 2010; Castelli. et al., 2014; Zhu. et al., 2013). Although PET has been used for measuring tissue pH since the 1970s, an inaccurate extracellular pH measurement may result. Meanwhile, MRS and MRI are limited in resolution. Fluorescent imaging and sensing provides a sensitive approach for determination of extracellular pH. Herein, we developed novel 2D ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor for imaging and tracking of tumor cells proliferation and metabolism, as illustrated in Scheme 1. Three new strategies were developed in the present work. Firstly, fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) was employed as the specific organic molecule for H+ (Shi. et al., 2012; Du. et al., 2013), and Nile blue chloride (Nile) was selected as the reference element for providing a built-in correction to avoid environmental effects (Zhu. et al., 2013). Both FITC and Nile were then conjugated onto the substrate surface of carbon quantum dots (CDs) to form CD@FITC-Nile fluorescent pH probes. The fluorescence emission peak at 515nm ascribed to FITC gradually increased with the increasing pH values, while that 670nm attributed to Nile almost stayed constant, leading to the ratiometric determination of pH. Next, CD@FITC-Nile probes were subsequently immobilized into a biocompatible sodium alginate hydrogel to prevent leakage of particles, thus constructing 2D ratiometric CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor with thickness of 500μm. The 2D ratiometric pH sensor showed broad dynamic range from pH 6.0 to 9.0. Finally, the present sensor which can detect pH over a wide proton concentration range with high accuracy and good biocompatibility, was successfully applied in real-time tracking extracellular pH changes in tumor cells proliferation and metabolism.

Graphite rods were bought from Alfa Aesar. Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT), FITC, Nile, amino acids (99%), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Sigma. Sodium alginate was purchased from Aladin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Metal ions were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Amino acids, monopotassium phosphate, dipotassium phosphate, and solution of metal ions were all prepared from their chloride salts. Dialysis tube (MWCO: 3500) was obtained from Ebioeasy Corporation. All chemicals are commercially available and of analytical grade. Ultrapure water was used from a Millipore water purification system.

Absorption spectrum was conducted on UV–vis spectrometer (Agilent 8453). Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL 2100, Japan) and AFM (Picoscan 2100 MI, USA) images were employed to observe the morphology of CDs. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700, Thermo Electron, America) was used to characterize the conjugation of CDs, FITC, and Nile. Spectrofluorimeter (F-2700FL, Hitachi, Japan) equipped with 90W xenon lamp was used to measure the fluorescence spectrum in a 1cm×1cm quartz cuvette. For the Gel scanning, CD@FITC-Nile@Gel plated on the petri dish was measured by confocal laser microscope (FV1000, Olympus, Japan) equipped with 10×objective. The green channel was collected from 500 to 560nm while the red channel was collected from 620 to 720nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs, CD@ FITC-Nile, and CD@FITC-Nile@gel
ce:section.ce:para: NaOH (0.3g) was dissolved in ethanol/H2O (99.5:0.5, v/v, 100mL) as the electrolyte solution and CDs were obtained with an electrolytic current intensity of 45mAcm−2. MgSO4 (4g) was added into the CDs solution with stirring for 30min, then stored overnight to desiccate water. Afterwards the solution was filtered to remove the solid salts. The raw CDs were re-dispersed in water, then centrifuged and dialyzed to remove large and small particles respectively. Finally, in order to obtain more carboxyl groups on the surface, CDs were refluxed in nitric acid for 24h.

For preparation of CD@Nile-FITC probe, FITC was mixed with ethanediamine (EDA) (1:1, mol/mol) at the pH of 8~9 and vigorously stirred for 24h in the dark. Then, EDC and NHS were added into the as-prepared CDs solution and stirred for 0.5h. Followed that, FITC (10−5 M) and Nile (2×10−4 M) were added into the CDs solution for the conjugation. The mixture was dialyzed against purified water to remove excessive molecules and CD@Nile-FITC probes were finally obtained. The as-prepared CD@Nile-FITC probe was stored at 4°C refrigerator for further use.

In order to obtain CD@FITC-Nile@Gel, the sodium alginate was dissolved in CD@Nile-FITC solution at a concentration of 3% (w/v), then solution (15mL) was acutely stirred to obtain homogeneously mixed sol. Next, the obtained sol (150μL) was dropped on petri dish and then covered with solution of calcium chloride (1.0M) using pump spray bottle. The gelation of the sol was allowed for 0.5h, avoiding concussion. Then Gel was soaked in ultrapure water for 3 times to remove external probes and calcium chloride. Finally, a rounded transparent film with diameter ~13mm, thickness ~0.5mm was obtained after drying at 60°C for 20min (Hu. et al., 2013; Tokarev. et al., 2010; Qin. 2008). The as-prepared gel film was placed in a box with continuous UV light illumination, for inhibiting growth of bacteria. All the processes above were operated in gnotobasis.

HeLa cells cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) containing high glucose added with fetal bovine serum (10%), penicillin (100 unitsmL−1) and streptomycin (100μgmL−1) were placed above the CD@FITC-Nile@Gel film. Then the film was placed in humidified incubator with an atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air at 37°C for 36h for Hela cells' proliferation. Next, the culture media was removed and re-added DMEM (100μL) without any buffered solution. The fluorescence imaging was obtained with Olympus confocal laser scanning microscope with a 10×objective, using 488nm exciting light. The images of the FITC channel were collected in 500−560nm range while those of the Nile channel were obtained in 620−720nm range.

The cellular cytotoxicity of CD@FITC-Nile@Gel was tested on HeLa cells. Before MTT test, HeLa cells (∼3×105 cell/mL) were distributed within 96-well microliter plates to a total 100μL/well volume. The plates were maintained at 37°C in a 5% CO2/95% air incubator for 24h. Then, the culture medium was removed and the cells were incubated in culture medium containing the as-prepared CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sol (3%) for 24h and 48h. After that, each well was added the new culture medium (100μL) containing MTT (10μL, 5mgmL−1) and incubated for 5h to allow the formazan dye's formation. After removing the medium, DMSO (150μL) was added to each well to dissolve the formazan in a microplate photometer (Multiskan MK3). Absorbance was measured at 490nm. Cell viability values were determined according to the following formulae: cell viability (%)=mean of absorbance value of treatment group/mean absorbance value of control group×100% (Zhuang. et al., 2014; Fu. et al., 2013).

CDs were synthesized according to the reported electrochemical method as an initial step of our work (Li. et al., 2010; Zhu. et al. 2012; Gao. et al., 2014; Qu. et al., 2012). A typical TEM image ( Fig. 1A) shows that the as-prepared CDs were homogeneously dispersed with an average size of around 6–7nm. Further observation indicates that lattice spacing of ∼0.32nm accords with (002) spacing of graphite structures (Li. et al., 2010; Zhu. et al. 2012). The cross-sectional view of an AFM image (see Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information) demonstrates that the average thickness of CDs is ∼1.5nm. From the UV–vis spectrum (see Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information), the absorption peak of CDs was clearly observed at 250–300nm, which represents a typical absorption of an aromatic π system (Zhu. et al. 2012). FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to track the surface modification. As demonstrated in curve a, Fig. 1B, five peaks were obviously observed at 3445cm−1 (νO–H), 1727cm−1 (νC=O), 1583cm−1 (νCOO-), 1381cm−1 (νCOO-), and 1103cm−1 (νC-OH) (Li. et al., 2010; Zhu. et al. 2012; Gao et al., 2014; Qu. et al., 2012; Shao. et al., 2013). The presence of these hydrophilic groups including -COOH imparts CDs water-solubility and easy to be modified with the molecules with -NH2 group. FT-IR spectrum for FITC dye showed four characteristic peaks in 3070cm−1, 2038cm−1, 1610cm−1, 1473cm−1 in curve b, ascribing to vibration of O–H, N=C=S, C=O, and C–O–C, respectively (Ding and Tian, 2015). In curve c, Nile dye demonstrated three peaks at 3424cm−1, 1589cm−1, 640cm−1, which are attributed to vibration of N–H. Finally, four peaks were clearly obtained at 3151cm−1 (νN–H), 1701cm−1 (νC=O), 1642cm−1 (νCOO-), and 1558cm−1 (νC–N) in the FT-IR spectrum of CD@Nile-FITC (curve d), confirming the successful conjugation of FITC and Nile on the CDs surface (Zhu et al., 2013).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs, FITC, Nile, and CD@FITC-Nile are demonstrated in Fig. 1C. CDs showed a broad emission peak, which was overlapped with the fluorescence emission peak of FITC at ∼515nm. The fluorescence emission peak of Nile – the inner reference molecule was clearly observed at ∼675nm. After conjugated FITC and Nile on the surface of CDs, CD@FITC-Nile exhibited two distinctive fluorescence emission peaks located at 515nm and 675nm. Next, standard fluorescence pH titration was performed using CD@FITC-Nile probe. As demonstrated in Fig. 1D, the FITC fluorescence peak at 515nm obviously increased with the increasing pH value, while the Nile fluorescence peak at 675nm almost remained stable, resulting in the ratiometric determination in a broad linear range of 5.90–9.20. Furthermore, CDs showed negligible responses toward pH, indicating CDs played the role just as the substrate for attachment of FITC and Nile in this work.

Then, CD@FITC-Nile probes were transferred into sodium alginate to form CD@FITC-Nile@Gel. TEM images in Fig. 2A and B indicate that CD@FITC-Nile probes mainly located at the crosslink sites of the nanofibers. As a result, the aggregated probes reduce the density of crosslink with the nanofibers about 20nm in diameter. This observation was in a good agreement with the rheological data given in Fig. S3 in Supporting Information. Frequency dependence of the modulus for blank gel and CD@FITC-Nile@Gel was tested by rheological method to confirm the elastic nature of hydrogel with and without probes. It can be seen that the dynamic storage modulus (G’) is greater than the loss modulus (G”), confirming the situation of gel. The G’ of CD@FITC-Nile@Gel is much lower than that of blank gel, demonstrating that the interaction of probes with the nanofibers in molecular hydrogel decreases the density of the crosslink (Yang. et al., 2008; Kiyonaka. et al., 2004; Wang. et al., 2007; Zhou. et al., 2014). Furthermore, confocal microscopy was employed to confirm whether CD@FITC-Nile@Gel was immobilized in the molecular hydrogel. From the fluorescent images shown in Fig. 2C and D, the 3D image, it can be clearly see that CD@FITC-Nile probes were stably immobilized in the hydrogel and mainly located at the crosslink sites of the nanofibers, which agrees with the rheological and TEM data. For the control experiment, no fluorescence was observed for blank gel, suggesting the fluorescent image observed in Fig. 2C and D is ascribed to the immobilization of CD@FITC-Nile probes in the hydrogel. CD@FITC-Nile@Gel showed two distintive emission peaks at ∼510nm and ∼670nm (see Fig. S4 in Supporting Information). The fluorescence intensity ratio (I green/I red) between green channel (I green: 500–560nm) and red channel (I red: 620–720nm) increased with the rising pH values, and demonstrated a good linearity in the range of 6.0-9.0, as plotted in Fig. 3 A. The linear range is relatively wide, and meets the requirements for imaging and biosensing of pH in living system.

For further application in the complex cellular environments, selectivity of CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor for pH determination against interferences from metal ions and amino acids was examined. The selectivity experiments were carried out by monitoring the fluorescence intensity ratio (I green/I red) of the sensor toward different pH (5.0, 7.4, and 9.0) in the presence of various metal ions and amino acids, which may coexist in the biological system. Remarkably, as shown in Fig. 3B, no obvious changes of the signal were observed in the presence of 1mM Na+, K+, Ca2+ and 10μm Mg2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and Cu2+ toward different pH. As shown in Fig. 3C, little effects were observed on the intensity ratio of the sensor after their exposure to 10μM concentration of typical amino acids including cysteine (Cys), glutamic acid (Glu), valine (Val), isoleucine (Iso), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), methionine (Meth), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), and histidine (His). All these experiments indicated high selectivity of this ratiometric hydrogel sensor for determination of pH against metal ions and amino acids due to the specific recognition of FITC toward H+. It is worth mentioning that the pH sensor displayed an excellent reversibility between pH 4.5, 7.4 and 9.0 (Fig. 3D), which shows the advantages of reused and renewed abilities of the sensor.

In addition, photostability of the CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4A, no obvious changes (< 5%) of the fluorescent responses (I green/I red) were observed after irradiated at 488nm for 1h, indicating good photostability of the Gel sensor. More importantly, the ratiometric fluorescent ratio remained almost unchanged under different oxygen and temperature conditions (Fig. 4B and C). These results demonstrate the long-term stability of this CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor against light illumination, oxygen, and temperature changes, which are very important features in terms of practicability.

The CD-based fluorescent probes have a good dispersibility in water and can be evenly distributed in a hydrogel layer. Since this matrix contains a large fraction of water, protons can easily transfer in and out. The CD-based fluorescent probes respond to pH changes within 10s. When using the CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor with thickness of ∼500μm, diffusion is slower and the response time was estimated to less than 5min, (see Fig. S5 in Supporting Information), and pH change from 6.0 to 9.0. Although the response of this Gel sensor is not so rapid compared to other pH sensors, the CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor is sufficient to track cells proliferation and metabolism. Leakage tests (see Fig. S6 in Supporting Information) indicated that neither the carbon dots nor the fluorescent dyes including FITC and Nile leached out of the hydrogel matrix on exposure to water over 48h.

As demonstrated above, the CD@FITC-Nile@Gel sensor showed high analytical performation for pH changes, as well as good reverbility and long-term stability, which are very promising for real-time pH imaging and biosensing in live system. For this purpose, the cell toxicity of CD@FITC-Nile@Gel towards HeLa cells was measured by means of a standard MTT assay. As summarized in Fig. 4D, the cellular viabilities were estimated to be greater than ~94% and ∼92% after incubation for 24h and 48h in the presence of CD@FITC-Nile probes with high concentration up to 0.1mgmL−1, and the sodium alginate sol with 3% concentration. The results suggest low cytotoxicity of the present CD@Nile-FITC@Gel sensor.

The 2D fluorescent sensor was then applied to tracking pH changes in the process of cells proliferation and metabolism. Hela cells were cultured on the pH-sensitive Gel sensor and pH changes during cells proliferation and metabolism were real-time monitored by ratiometric fluorescent imaging. As shown in Fig. 5A, the 2D ratiometric fluorescent sensor displayed a homogeneous green fluorescence (pseudo-color) and the ratio between green channel intensity and red channel intensity (Igreen/Ired) was estimated to ∼3.57±0.20 (Fig. 5B), revealing a starting pH value of ∼7.2. With cells proliferation and metabolism, the green fluorescence gradually dimmed, while the red fluorescence stayed almost unchanged. Meanwhile, the intensity ratio of green channel and red one obviously decreased with the time of cells proliferation and metabolism, and turned into 1.93±0.11 up to 16h (Fig. 5B). This result suggests that after 16h proliferation and metabolism, extracellular pH dropped from ∼7.2 to ∼6.5.

To conclude, we have developed a CD-based 2D ratiometric fluorescence sensor for imaging and biosensing pH changes during cells proliferation and metabolism. The present ratiometric Gel sensor showed high selectivity and accuracy for determination of pH in a broad linear range of 6.0–9.0, due to the specific recognition element – FITC and inner reference molecule – Nile. Furthermore, the CD-based Gel sensor demonstrated long-term stability again light illumination, oxygen, and temperature changes, as well as good reversibility and low cytotoxicity. Accordingly, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor was successfully applied to tracking pH changes during cells proliferation and metabolism, which may be connected with biological and pathological processes. The developed method can be extended to real-time imaging and sensing other parameters, for example oxygen, carbon dioxide, and biosensing multi-species. The work is underway in our laboratory.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by NSFC (21175098) and National Nature Science Fund for distinguished young scholars (21325521 for Y. Tian).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.03.033.
